title: Actions Cost Optimization
description: Strategies to minimize GitHub Actions billing costs through caching, runner selection, concurrency management, and cost monitoring. Analyze compute vs storage vs transfer cost drivers.

sections.title: Pricing Model
sections.description: GitHub Actions billing is based on compute minutes (per OS), storage (artifacts + caches), and data transfer. Linux minutes are the baseline; macOS and Windows incur 10× and 2× multipliers respectively.
sections.codeBlock:
# GitHub Actions billing rates (public cloud)
# Linux:    $0.008/min  (1×)
# Windows:  $0.016/min  (2×)
# macOS:    $0.080/min  (10×)
#
# Storage: $0.25/GB/month after 1 GB free (artifacts + caches)
# Transfer: $0.50–$0.90/GB depending on region
#
# Free tier (public repos: unlimited)
# Free tier (private repos):
#   2,000 min/month Linux
#   Equivalent to 200 min macOS or 1,000 min Windows
#   500 MB storage
#   1 GB transfer
#
# Compute cost = min_used × multiplier × $0.008
# Example: 500 min Windows = 500 × 2 × $0.008 = $8.00

sections.title: Caching Strategies
sections.description: Use actions/cache and actions/cache/save/restore to persist dependencies and build outputs across workflow runs. Cache keys use hash inputs to invalidate on change. Target >80% hit rate.
sections.codeBlock:
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Cache node_modules
        id: cache-npm
        uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: ~/.npm
          key: npm-${{ runner.os }}-${{ hashFiles('package-lock.json') }}
          restore-keys: |
            npm-${{ runner.os }}-
            npm-

      - name: Cache build output
        uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: |
            dist
            .next
          key: build-${{ github.sha }}
          restore-keys: |
            build-${{ github.ref }}-
            build-

      - if: steps.cache-npm.outputs.cache-hit != 'true'
        run: npm ci

sections.title: Runner Selection
sections.description: Match runner size to job requirements. Use smaller runners for linting, larger runners for builds. Self-hosted runners eliminate per-minute costs entirely but require infrastructure management.
sections.codeBlock:
jobs:
  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest  # free tier or cheap
    steps:
      - run: npm run lint

  test:
    strategy:
      matrix:
        os: [ubuntu-latest]  # avoid macOS unless needed
    runs-on: ${{ matrix.os }}
    steps:
      - run: npm test

  build:
    runs-on:
      group: larger-runner  # org-level larger runner
      labels: [self-hosted, linux, x64]
    # Self-hosted: $0/min compute cost
    # But: infrastructure, maintenance, network egress

  e2e:
    runs-on: macos-latest  # only if macOS required
    # $0.08/min — use sparingly
    steps:
      - run: npm run test:e2e

# Label routing: assign runner pools per job criticality
# high-priority → dedicated runner group
# low-priority → shared group with queue

sections.title: Concurrency Management
sections.description: Use concurrency groups to prevent redundant builds (cancel duplicate pushes) and limit parallel jobs. Set max-parallel on matrix strategies to control burst spend.
sections.codeBlock:
name: CI
on:
  push:
    branches-ignore:
      - 'dependabot/**'  # skip dependabot if desired

concurrency:
  group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: ${{ github.ref != 'refs/heads/main' }}

jobs:
  test:
    strategy:
      matrix:
        os: [ubuntu-latest, windows-latest]
        node: [18, 20, 22]
      max-parallel: 4  # max concurrent matrix jobs
    runs-on: ${{ matrix.os }}
    timeout-minutes: 30  # prevent runaway jobs
    steps:
      - run: npm ci && npm test

# Further controls:
# - timeout-minutes: hard limit per job
# - scheduled workflows: off-peak hours
# - path filters: only run on relevant changes
#   on:
#     push:
#       paths:
#         - 'src/**'
#         - '*.json'

sections.title: Cost Monitoring
sections.description: Track costs via the GitHub billing API, organization usage graphs, and webhook events. Set budgets and alerts for unexpected spend spikes. Use github-script to automate cost reporting.

details: GitHub Actions cost optimization requires visibility into three dimensions: compute, storage, and transfer. Compute dominates (~65% of bill) with Linux at $0.008/min as baseline — macOS at 10× should only be used for iOS/macOS-specific builds. Caching is the highest-leverage optimization: a well-configured cache with >80% hit rate can eliminate 60-70% of dependency install time. Use hashFiles() on lockfiles for dependency caches and build artifact caching with restore-keys for partial-match fallback. Cache eviction follows LRU with a 10 GB repository limit — prioritize key quality over cache size. Self-hosted runners eliminate per-minute billing entirely but shift cost to infrastructure (EC2, VMs, Kubernetes) and operational overhead (updates, patching, networking). Concurrency groups with cancel-in-progress prevent wasted minutes from superseded pushes, and max-parallel caps matrix job burst. Path filtering on workflow triggers ensures compute is only consumed for relevant changes. Monitor costs weekly via the billing API and set up automated alerts for anomalous spend spikes.
cheatSheet:
Core Commands

  • concurrency: group: ci group: ci cancel-in-progress: true — cancel redundant runs.
  • actions/cache@v4 — cache dependencies across runs.
  • self-hosted runner — free compute, no per-minute billing.

Staff+ Patterns

  • Use paths-ignore and paths-filter to skip CI on docs-only or non-source changes — biggest quick cost save.
  • Cache ~/.npm, ~/.cache/pip, and node_modules with language-specific keys including hashFiles for exact restoration.

Watch Outs

  • Self-hosted runners still cost maintenance time. Factor in admin overhead vs. per-minute savings.
  • Cache restore is not guaranteed (cache eviction, key mismatch). Always fall back to npm ci.

Interview Angle

Cost optimization is a Staff+ concern — show you think about the bottom line. Discuss trade-offs: faster CI (more parallelism) vs cheaper CI (conservative parallelism, caching, self-hosted).
